s: The Czech word 'led' means ice.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'led'?
e: Led (pronounced 'led') is the Czech word for ice. Example: 'Ona si vezme jablečný džus s ledem.' (She will have apple juice with ice.)

s: The Czech word 'velký' means big or large.
q: What does the Czech adjective 'velký' mean?
e: Velký (pronounced 'vel-kee') is the Czech adjective meaning big or large. Example: 'Ty si vezmeš velkou sklenici piva.' (You will take a large glass of beer.)

s: The Czech word 'střední' means medium.
q: What is the Czech word for medium size?
e: Střední (pronounced 'strzhe-nee') is the Czech adjective for medium size. Example: 'On si vzal střední velikost kávy.' (He took a medium size coffee.)

s: The Czech word 'pivo' means beer.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'pivo'?
e: Pivo (pronounced 'pee-vo') is the Czech word for beer. Czech Republic is famous for its beer culture. Example: 'Můj otec pije kávu, ale nepije pivo.' (My father drinks coffee but doesn't drink beer.)

s: The Czech phrase 'červené víno' means red wine.
q: What does the Czech phrase 'červené víno' translate to?
e: Červené víno (pronounced 'cher-ve-nay vee-no') means red wine in Czech. Example: 'Já nepiji pivo, ale piji červené víno.' (I don't drink beer, but I drink red wine.)

s: The Czech phrase 'bílé víno' means white wine.
q: What is the English translation of 'bílé víno'?
e: Bílé víno (pronounced 'bee-lay vee-no') means white wine. Example: 'Ona nechtěla červené víno, ale bílé víno.' (She didn't want red wine, but white wine.)

s: The Czech word 'alkohol' means alcohol.
q: What does the Czech word 'alkohol' mean?
e: Alkohol (pronounced 'al-ko-hol') is the Czech word for alcohol. Example: 'Ona vůbec nepije alkohol.' (She doesn't drink alcohol at all.)

s: The Czech word 'láhev' means bottle.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'láhev'?
e: Láhev (pronounced 'la-hev') is the Czech word for bottle. Example: 'Lahvové pivo prosím!' (Bottled beer please!)

s: The Czech preposition 'bez' means without.
q: What does the Czech word 'bez' mean?
e: Bez (pronounced 'bez') is the Czech preposition meaning without. Example: 'Ovocný džus bez cukru.' (Fruit juice without sugar.)

s: The Czech phrase 'ledová káva' means iced coffee.
q: What is the English translation of 'ledová káva'?
e: Ledová káva (pronounced 'le-do-va ka-va') means iced coffee. Example: 'On má rád velkou sklenici ledové kávy.' (He likes a large glass of iced coffee.)

s: The Czech word 'trochu' means a little or a bit.
q: What does the Czech word 'trochu' mean?
e: Trochu (pronounced 'tro-hu') means a little or a bit. Example: 'Ta káva je trochu sladká.' (The coffee is a little sweet.)

s: The Czech adverb 'velmi' means very.
q: What does the Czech word 'velmi' mean?
e: Velmi (pronounced 'vel-mi') is the Czech adverb meaning very. Example: 'Ta ledová káva je velice sladká.' (The iced coffee is very sweet.)

s: The Czech phrase 'citrónový čaj' means lemon tea.
q: What is the English translation of 'citrónový čaj'?
e: Citrónový čaj (pronounced 'tsi-tro-no-vee chai') means lemon tea. Example: 'Ona ráda pije citrónový čaj bez cukru.' (She likes to drink lemon tea without sugar.)

s: The Czech phrase 'zelený čaj' means green tea.
q: What does the Czech phrase 'zelený čaj' mean?
e: Zelený čaj (pronounced 'ze-le-nee chai') means green tea. Example: 'On rád pije zelený čaj.' (He likes to drink green tea.)

s: The Czech word 'mléko' means milk.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'mléko'?
e: Mléko (pronounced 'mle-ko') is the Czech word for milk. Example: 'Já nerada piji mléko.' (I don't like to drink milk.)

s: The Czech verb 'vstoupit' means to come in or to enter.
q: What does the Czech verb 'vstoupit' mean?
e: Vstoupit (pronounced 'vstou-pit') is the Czech infinitive verb meaning to come in or to enter. Example: 'Prosím, vstupte.' (Please come in.)

s: The Czech verb 'sednout' means to sit.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech verb 'sednout'?
e: Sednout (pronounced 'sed-nout') is the Czech infinitive verb meaning to sit. Example: 'Prosím, sedněte si tady.' (Please sit here.)

s: The Czech verb 'objednat' means to order.
q: What does the Czech verb 'objednat' mean?
e: Objednat (pronounced 'ob-ye-dnat') is the Czech infinitive verb meaning to order. Example: 'Prosím, objednej jídlo.' (Please order food.)

s: The Czech verb 'počkat' means to wait.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech verb 'počkat'?
e: Počkat (pronounced 'poch-kat') is the Czech infinitive verb meaning to wait. Example: 'Prosím, počkej moment.' (Please wait a moment.)

s: The Czech word 'lístek' means a menu.
q: What does the Czech word 'lístek' mean?
e: Lístek (pronounced 'lis-tek') is the Czech word for menu. It can also mean a ticket or a small piece of paper. Example: 'Jídlení lístek prosím.' (Menu please.)

s: The Czech phrase 'být hladový' means to be hungry.
q: What is the English translation of 'být hladový'?
e: Být hladový (pronounced 'bee-ti hla-do-vee') means to be hungry in Czech. Example: 'Já jsem velmi hladový.' (I am very hungry.)

s: The Czech adjective 'sytý' means full or satisfied after eating.
q: What does the Czech word 'sytý' mean?
e: Sytý (pronounced 'si-tee') is the Czech adjective meaning full or satiated. Example: 'Já jsem sytý.' (I am full.)

s: The Czech phrase 'být alergický' means to be allergic.
q: What is the English translation of 'být alergický'?
e: Být alergický (pronounced 'bee-ti a-ler-gi-tskee') means to be allergic. Example: 'On je alergický na mořské plody.' (He is allergic to seafood.)

s: The Czech phrase 'být vegetarián' means to be vegetarian.
q: What does the Czech phrase 'být vegetarián' mean?
e: Být vegetarián (pronounced 'bee-ti ve-ge-ta-ri-an') means to be vegetarian. Example: 'Ona je vegetariánka.' (She is a vegetarian.)

s: The Czech phrase 'být na dietě' means to be on a diet.
q: What is the English translation of 'být na dietě'?
e: Být na dietě (pronounced 'bee-ti na di-e-tye') means to be on a diet. Example: 'Ona je na dietě.' (She is on a diet.)

s: The Czech word 'více' means more.
q: What does the Czech word 'více' mean?
e: Více (pronounced 'vee-tse') is the Czech word meaning more. Example: 'Já bych si chtěl objednat více jídla.' (I would like to order more food.)

s: The Czech word 'svačina' means a snack.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'svačina'?
e: Svačina (pronounced 'sva-chi-na') is the Czech word for a snack or light meal. Example: 'Já bych chtěl nějakou svačinu.' (I would like a snack.)

s: The Czech word 'dezert' means dessert.
q: What does the Czech word 'dezert' mean?
e: Dezert (pronounced 'de-zert') is the Czech word for dessert. Example: 'Já bych chtěl jako dezert ovoce.' (I would like fruit as dessert.)

s: The Czech word 'nápoj' means a drink.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'nápoj'?
e: Nápoj (pronounced 'na-poy') is the Czech word for a drink or beverage. Example: 'Chtěl bys vidět nápojový lístek.' (Would you like to see the drink menu?)

s: The Czech word 'zmrzlina' means ice cream.
q: What does the Czech word 'zmrzlina' mean?
e: Zmrzlina (pronounced 'zmrzli-na') is the Czech word for ice cream. Example: 'Oni by si chtěli objednat zmrzlinu.' (They would like to order ice cream.)

s: The Czech verb 'platit' means to pay.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech verb 'platit'?
e: Platit (pronounced 'pla-tit') is the Czech infinitive verb meaning to pay. Example: 'Já musím teď platit.' (I have to pay now.)

s: The Czech phrase 'kreditní karta' means a credit card.
q: What does the Czech phrase 'kreditní karta' mean?
e: Kreditní karta (pronounced 'kre-dit-nee kar-ta') means credit card in Czech. Example: 'Mohu platit kreditní kartou?' (Can I pay with a credit card?)

s: The Czech verb 'doporučit' means to recommend.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech verb 'doporučit'?
e: Doporučit (pronounced 'do-po-ru-chit') is the Czech infinitive verb meaning to recommend. Example: 'Mohu Vám doporučit pokrm?' (May I recommend a dish for you?)

s: The Czech verb 'přinést' means to bring or to have (get).
q: What does the Czech verb 'přinést' mean?
e: Přinést (pronounced 'pzhi-nest') is the Czech infinitive verb meaning to bring or to get. Example: 'Můžete mi přinést jídelní lístek v angličtině?' (Can you bring me a menu in English?)

s: The Czech word 'účet' means a bill or an account.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'účet'?
e: Účet (pronounced 'u-chet') is the Czech word for a bill or account. In a restaurant context, it means the bill. Example: 'Mohl by jste mi prosím přinést účet?' (Could you please bring me the bill?)

s: The Czech word 'účtenka' means a receipt.
q: What does the Czech word 'účtenka' mean?
e: Účtenka (pronounced 'u-chen-ka') is the Czech word for a receipt. Example: 'Mohl by jste mi prosím přinést účtenku?' (Could you please give me a receipt?)

s: The Czech preposition 'pod' means under.
q: What does the Czech word 'pod' mean?
e: Pod (pronounced 'pod') is the Czech preposition meaning under. Example: 'Ta kniha je pod krabicí s jahodami.' (The book is under the box with strawberries.)

s: The Czech word 'pokoj' means a room.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'pokoj'?
e: Pokoj (pronounced 'po-koy') is the Czech word for a room. Example: 'Ten dámský časopis je v pokoji.' (The women's magazine is in the room.)

s: The Czech word 'stůl' means a table.
q: What does the Czech word 'stůl' mean?
e: Stůl (pronounced 'stool') is the Czech word for table. Example: 'Stůl je v pokoji.' (The table is in the room.)

s: The Czech preposition 'na' means on or to.
q: What does the Czech preposition 'na' mean?
e: Na (pronounced 'na') is the Czech preposition meaning on or to. Example: 'Jablka jsou na stole.' (The apples are on the table.)

s: The Czech word 'slovník' means a dictionary.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'slovník'?
e: Slovník (pronounced 'slov-nik') is the Czech word for a dictionary. Example: 'Anglický slovník je na stole.' (The English dictionary is on the table.)

s: The Czech word 'křeslo' means a chair or armchair.
q: What does the Czech word 'křeslo' mean?
e: Křeslo (pronounced 'kzhes-lo') is the Czech word for a chair or armchair. Example: 'On sedí v křesle.' (He is sitting in the chair.)

s: The Czech preposition 'mezi' means between.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'mezi'?
e: Mezi (pronounced 'me-zi') is the Czech preposition meaning between. Example: 'Ten slovník je mezi novinami a časopisem.' (The dictionary is between the newspaper and the magazine.)

s: The Czech word 'pero' means a pen.
q: What does the Czech word 'pero' mean?
e: Pero (pronounced 'pe-ro') is the Czech word for a pen. Example: 'To pero je mezi sklenicí a slovníkem.' (The pen is between the glass and the dictionary.)

s: The Czech word 'učebna' means a classroom.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'učebna'?
e: Učebna (pronounced 'u-cheb-na') is the Czech word for a classroom. Example: 'V učebně jsou křesla.' (There are chairs in the classroom.)

s: The Czech word 'učitel' means a teacher.
q: What does the Czech word 'učitel' mean?
e: Učitel (pronounced 'u-chi-tel') is the Czech word for a teacher. Example: 'Učitel sedí mezi mnou a mým spolužákem.' (The teacher sits between me and my classmate.)

s: The Czech preposition 'mimo' means outside.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech word 'mimo'?
e: Mimo (pronounced 'mi-mo') is the Czech preposition meaning outside. Example: 'Učitel je mimo učebnu.' (The teacher is outside the classroom.)

s: The Czech preposition 'uvnitř' means inside.
q: What does the Czech word 'uvnitř' mean?
e: Uvnitř (pronounced 'u-vi-trzh') is the Czech preposition meaning inside. Example: 'Všichni studenti jsou uvnitř učebny.' (All students are inside the classroom.)

s: The Czech preposition 've' means at or in.
q: What is the English translation of the Czech preposition 've'?
e: Ve (pronounced 've') is the Czech preposition meaning at or in. It is often used before words starting with v or f. Example: 'Já jsem ve škole.' (I am at school.)

s: The Czech word 'škola' means a school.
q: What does the Czech word 'škola' mean?
e: Škola (pronounced 'shko-la') is the Czech word for a school. Example: 'Studenti jsou ve škole.' (The students are at school.)
